school: Abu Abdullah Rudaki (858–941), the ‘Father of Persian Poetry’ who served at the Samanid court in Bukhara. He was the first major poet to write in New Persian, establishing the literary tradition that would produce Firdausi, Rumi, and Hafiz. Reportedly blind from birth, his mastery of the qasida and ghazal forms set the standard for centuries of Persian literature.
bio: A 9th-10th century Persian poet and musician considered one of the greatest early Persian poets, credited with pioneering the New Persian literary tradition after the Arab conquest.

school: Abu al-Tayyib al-Mutanabbi (915–965), widely regarded as the greatest Arab poet. He served at the Hamdanid court in Aleppo under Sayf al-Dawla, producing panegyrics of unmatched power. His nickname means ‘the would-be prophet,’ reflecting his extraordinary self-confidence. His verse combines complex imagery, philosophical depth, and rhetorical brilliance that remains unsurpassed in Arabic literature.
quotes: I am known to the night, to the horse, to the desert; known to the sword, to the spear, to paper and pen.
I am known to the night and to the horse and to the desert; known to the sword, the lance, the parchment and the pen.
The noblest of all aims is the one a man sets for himself when there is nothing left to prove except his own worth.
The sword is more truthful than books — its edge is the boundary between seriousness and play.
On the face of every brave man there is a scar — and in the eyes of every coward there is a lie.

school: Abu al-Qasim Firdowsi (940–1020) spent over thirty years composing the Shahnameh (Book of Kings), the Persian national epic spanning 50,000 couplets. Writing for the Ghaznavid court, he preserved pre-Islamic Persian mythology and history in verse, effectively reviving the Persian language and cultural identity after the Arab conquest.

Baba Kuhi of Shiraz (948–1037) was a Persian Sufi poet and mystic revered as the patron saint of Shiraz. His tomb on a hilltop overlooking the city has been a site of pilgrimage for nearly a thousand years. He was a contemporary of the great Sufi Abu Sa'id ibn Abi'l-Khayr and is said to have met him during his travels in Khorasan. His Persian poetry, collected in a small diwan, expresses the ecstatic union of the lover with the divine Beloved in language that anticipates the great Persian Sufi poets who followed. His most famous line — 'In the market, in the cloister, only God I saw' — captures the essence of the Sufi doctrine of wahdat al-shuhud (unity of witness). He spent long years in ascetic retreat on the mountain above Shiraz before his death at a great age.
bio: A 10th-century Persian Sufi poet and ascetic from the Khorasan tradition, known for mystical poetry and spiritual teachings that influenced Persian Sufism.

school: Persian Sufi master and poet (967–1049) from Khorasan who pioneered ecstatic Sufi gatherings with music and poetry (sama’). He was among the first to compose Persian rubai’at (quatrains) on mystical themes. His rule for communal Sufi life became a model for later Sufi lodges across the Islamic world.
bio: A renowned 10th-11th century Persian Sufi poet and mystic from the Khorasan region, pioneering the rubai form and known for his spiritual poetry celebrating divine love and ascetic virtue.
quotes: I have not seen a single person more righteous than one who bears the burden of others

school: Twelver Shia poet and scholar of Baghdad (970–1016) who compiled the Nahj al-Balagha, the collected sermons, letters, and sayings of Imam Ali — one of the most important texts in Shia Islam. He was also an accomplished poet in his own right, producing some of the finest Arabic verse of his era.
quotes: Poetry is the highest form of the Arabic language — it is where it reaches its limit.
The noble man carries his grief without display — he weeps in private and smiles in public.
I compiled the words of Ali not to glorify him but to preserve what wisdom looks like.
Poetry is the highest form of the Arabic language — it is where it reaches its limit.
The noble man carries his grief without display — he weeps in private and smiles in public.
I compiled the words of Ali not to glorify him but to preserve what wisdom looks like.

school: An eleventh- and twelfth-century Persian polymath from Nishapur, he made lasting contributions to mathematics, astronomy, and philosophy, though he is best known in the West for the Rubaiyat, a collection of quatrains popularised by Edward FitzGerald's nineteenth-century English translation. In mathematics, he developed a geometric method for solving cubic equations and contributed to the reform of the Persian calendar, producing one of the most accurate calendars in history. His philosophical poetry explores themes of mortality, fate, and the pursuit of earthly pleasures with characteristic wit and scepticism.

books.title: The Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam (FitzGerald translation)
books.note: Famous English translation of Persian quatrains — Project Gutenberg

quotes: Be happy for this moment. This moment is your life.
Be happy for this moment — this moment is your life.
I sent my soul through the invisible, some letter of that after-life to spell — and by and by my soul returned to me and answered: I myself am heaven and hell.

school: Persian Sufi poet (1080–1131) from the Ghaznavid court who pioneered the use of the mathnawi form for mystical-didactic poetry. His Hadiqat al-Haqiqa (Garden of Truth) was the first great Persian mystical epic, directly inspiring Attar and Rumi. Rumi called Sanai and Attar his two ‘soul-parents.’

books.title: The Enclosed Garden of the Truth (Hadiqat al-Haqiqa)

quotes: Until man destroys the self, he cannot pass beyond it.
The wine of divine love has no dregs — drink it and remain forever awake.
The lover’s religion is love itself — his creed is the Beloved.
Reason is the guide to the door of love — but it cannot enter with you.
The eye that weeps for God sees more clearly than the eye that has never wept.
The lover’s religion is love itself — his creed is the Beloved.
Reason is the guide to the door of love — but it cannot enter with you.
The eye that weeps for God sees more clearly than the eye that has never wept.

school: Azerbaijani-Persian Poetry — His Khamsa became the model for Persian romantic epic poetry across the Muslim world
quotes: Love is that flame which, when it blazes up, burns away everything except the Beloved.

school: Persian Sufi poet of Nishapur (c. 1145–1221) whose Conference of the Birds (Mantiq al-Tayr) is the greatest allegorical poem in Persian literature. In it, thirty birds journey to find the Simurgh (God) and discover that they themselves are the Simurgh. Rumi called Attar one of his two ‘soul-parents.’ He was reportedly killed during the Mongol invasion of Nishapur.

books.title: Mantiq al-Tayr (Conference of the Birds)
books.note: One of the greatest allegories in world literature — the soul's journey to God through the 7 valleys.

books.title: The Conference of the Birds (Mantiq al-Tayr)

quotes: The heart is the secret inside the secret inside the secret.
Be nothing — then you will be everything.
The bird that does not fly toward the Simurgh has not understood why it has wings.
Every soul is on a journey — the only question is whether it knows its destination.
Love consumed me so completely that nothing remained of me but love itself.
The bird that does not fly toward the Simurgh has not understood why it has wings.
Every soul is on a journey — the only question is whether it knows its destination.
Love consumed me so completely that nothing remained of me but love itself.

school: Arabic Sufi poetry — the greatest Arabic mystical poet; lived between Cairo and Mecca, his Qasida al-Khamriyya (Wine Ode) and Nazm al-Suluk (Poem of the Way) are the supreme achievements of Arabic Sufi literature
quotes: In my intoxication I do not know whether it is you in me or I in you.
Before the creation of the vine, I was drunk on a wine that was never pressed.
In love, no intermediary stands between the lover and the Beloved.
I drank, and the wine drank me — and the cup passed between us with no witness but Oneness.
My heart beats only to the rhythm of longing.

school: Shirazi School / Persian Classical Poetry — His poem on human brotherhood is inscribed at the UN Human Rights Council

books.title: Gulistan (Rose Garden)
books.note: Classic of ethical wisdom and Persian prose

quotes: Human beings are members of a whole, in creation of one essence and soul.
Have patience. All things are difficult before they become easy.
Human beings are members of a whole — in creation of one essence and soul.

school: Shadhiliyya — Egyptian poet who wrote the Burda; the most recited poem in Islamic history; said to have been healed of paralysis through the Prophet
quotes: You are the sun and the other prophets are the stars — their light is hidden when yours appears.
My soul has become attached to the hope of your intercession — and hope does not disappoint the one who truly hopes.

school: Shadhiliyya — Andalusian poet who popularised Sufi poetry in colloquial Arabic; bridge between Ibn Sab'in and al-Shadhili
bio: A 13th-century Egyptian Sufi poet and disciple of the Shadhiliyya order, known for his spiritual devotional poetry and mystical verses celebrating divine union.

school: Persian Sufi poet (1213–1289) who studied under both Suhrawardi masters and the circle of Ibn Arabi in Damascus. His Lama’at (Divine Flashes) merges the philosophical mysticism of Ibn Arabi with Persian lyrical beauty. He was a bridge between Andalusian theoretical Sufism and the Persian poetic tradition.

books.title: Divine Flashes (Lama'at)
books.note: Sufi love poetry influenced by Ibn Arabi — English

school: Anatolian Turkish Sufi poet (c. 1238–1320) who wrote in simple, vernacular Turkish rather than the courtly Persian or Arabic of his era. His poems of divine love, unity, and compassion laid the foundation for Turkish literary and spiritual identity. He is arguably the most beloved poet in Turkish culture.
quotes: I am not here to be an enemy to anyone — to me the whole world is one.
The one who has not tasted love does not know what the soul is.
Let me die before I die so that when I die I do not die.
I am not here to be an enemy to anyone — to me the whole world is one.
Let me die before I die so that when I die I do not die.
The one who has not tasted love does not know what the soul is.

school: Chishti Order / Mughal Pre-history — Spiritual disciple of Nizamuddin Auliya; credited with inventing the tabla and developing qawwali
quotes: I am the parrot of India — and India is my cage made of gold.
Music is the hidden prayer of the soul — it reaches God when words cannot.
Every language is a door to God — and I have tried to open as many as I can.
I am the parrot of India — and India is my cage made of gold.
Music is the hidden prayer of the soul — it reaches God when words cannot.
Every language is a door to God — and I have tried to open as many as I can.

school: Persian Sufi poet (1288–1340) whose Gulshan-i Raz (Rose Garden of Mystery) is one of the most widely read works of Persian mystical verse. Written as a response to fifteen questions about the nature of reality, it expounds the doctrine of wahdat al-wujud (Unity of Being) in accessible poetic form.
bio: Persian Sufi poet and mystic influenced by Ibn Arabi's Akbarian philosophy, best known for his mystical poem 'The Secret Garden.' He was a significant figure in Persian Sufi literature.

school: Khwaja Shams-ud-Din Muhammad Hafez-i Shirazi (1315–1390) is the supreme master of the Persian ghazal and the most beloved poet in the Persian-speaking world. His Divan, used for bibliomancy across Iran, combines profoundly ambiguous mystical symbolism with exquisite lyrical beauty. Goethe called him an equal, and his tomb in Shiraz remains a pilgrimage site.
quotes: Even after all this time the sun never says to the earth: you owe me. Look what happens with a love like that — it lights the whole world.
Even after all this time the sun never says to the earth: you owe me.
I wish I could show you the astonishing light of your own being.

school: Anatolian Sufi poet (c. 1341–1444) regarded as the greatest literary figure of the Alevi-Bektashi tradition. His poems combine folk humour with radical mystical egalitarianism, challenging social hierarchies and religious formalism. He wrote in simple Turkish accessible to common people, making him a voice for the marginalised.
bio: Turkish Qalandari Sufi poet and mystic known for his devotional and mystical verses. He was influential in the development of Turkish folk spirituality and poetry.

school: Last great classical Persian Sufi poet (1414–1492) who was also a Naqshbandi shaykh and scholar based in Herat. His Haft Awrang (Seven Thrones) and Baharistan are masterworks of Persian literature. He wrote over forty books spanning poetry, theology, and Sufi philosophy, and was a key commentator on the works of Ibn Arabi.
quotes: I have lived on the lip of insanity, wanting to know reasons, knocking on a door. It opens. I have been knocking from the inside.
I have lived on the lip of insanity, wanting to know reasons — knocking on a door. It opens. I have been knocking from the inside.
Friend, our closeness is this: anywhere you put your foot, feel me in the firmness under you.
Love is the astrolabe of God’s mysteries — whoever holds it rightly reads the heavens.
The friend of God needs no intermediary — he has already arrived.
Poetry is philosophy dressed in beauty — it teaches what argument cannot reach.

school: Timurid literary culture — the greatest Chagatai Turkic poet; companion of Jami; founded Turkic as a literary language
quotes: The Turkic tongue is no less noble than Arabic or Persian — God speaks to every people in their own language.
The poet who writes only for kings has sold his gift to the lowest buyer.
Language is the homeland of the soul — whoever masters his language has found himself.
The Turkic tongue is no less noble than Arabic or Persian — God speaks to every people in their own language.
The poet who writes only for kings has sold his gift to the lowest buyer.
Language is the homeland of the soul — whoever masters his language has found himself.

school: Alevi-Bektashi — the greatest folk-Sufi poet of Anatolia; executed for leading a rebellion; revered as a martyr
bio: Turkish Qalandari Sufi poet and folk figure known for his devotional and often politically subversive verses. He is revered in Turkish Alevi tradition as a spiritual master and poet.

school: Ottoman-Azeri poetry — greatest Azerbaijani and one of the greatest Ottoman poets; wrote in Turkish, Persian and Arabic
quotes: I sought a remedy for the pain of love and found that love itself is the remedy.
The lover does not complain of suffering — he complains only when love is absent.
Every poem I wrote was a prayer I did not know how to say any other way.
I sought a remedy for the pain of love and found that love itself is the remedy.
The lover does not complain of suffering — he complains only when love is absent.
Every poem I wrote was a prayer I did not know how to say any other way.

school: Mahmud Abd al-Baqi (1526–1600), known as Baki, was the greatest Ottoman divan poet and held the title Sultan üş-Şuara (Sultan of Poets). Born in Istanbul to a modest family, he was apprenticed to a saddler before his poetic talent was discovered by patrons who supported his education. He mastered the Persian-influenced ghazal form in Ottoman Turkish and brought it to its highest perfection, combining technical mastery with emotional depth. His elegy (mersiye) on the death of Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent is considered the finest poem in Ottoman literature. He served as a judge and professor while producing a relatively small but exquisite body of work — his Divan contains around 500 ghazals. Baki's poetry defined the golden age of Ottoman classical literature.
bio: A 16th-century Ottoman Turkish poet considered one of the greatest masters of the Ottoman lyric tradition, known for his gazels and refined courtly poetry.

school: Abu al-Faiz ibn Mubarak (1547–1595), known as Faizi, was the poet laureate of the Mughal Emperor Akbar and one of the 'Nine Jewels' (Navaratnas) of Akbar's court. He was the elder brother of Abu'l-Fazl ibn Mubarak, Akbar's chief minister and chronicler. Born in Agra, he became the leading Persian poet in India and tutored Akbar's sons. His most ambitious work was Nal Daman, a Persian retelling of the Sanskrit Nala and Damayanti story, composed entirely without dotted letters — a tour de force of constrained writing. He also translated mathematical and scientific texts from Sanskrit into Persian for Akbar's program of cultural synthesis. His early death at 48 was mourned by the entire Mughal court.
bio: A 16th-century Mughal poet and scholar, brother of the renowned philosopher Abul Fazl, known for his Persian and Hindi poetry and service to Emperor Akbar.

school: Wujudiyya Sufism — the first major Malay-language Sufi poet; brought Sufi metaphysics (Ibn Arabi) to Southeast Asia
bio: A 16th-century Malay Sufi poet and mystic associated with the Shadhiliyya order, known for pioneering Malay Islamic mystical poetry and spiritual teachings in Southeast Asia.

school: Ömer Nef'i (1572–1635) was the greatest Ottoman qasida (panegyric) poet and one of the most feared satirists in Ottoman literary history. Born in Hasankale near Erzurum, he came to Istanbul and won fame for his dazzling praise poems to sultans and viziers — and notoriety for his savage satirical attacks on those he despised. His collection Siham-ı Kaza (Arrows of Destiny) is a masterpiece of Ottoman satirical verse that earned him powerful enemies. His qasidas to Sultan Murad IV are considered the pinnacle of the Ottoman panegyric tradition, combining hyperbolic praise with genuine poetic grandeur. His satires eventually proved fatal: Grand Vizier Bayram Pasha had him strangled with a bowstring in 1635. He remains the supreme example of how Ottoman poetry could be both a path to power and a death sentence.
bio: Ottoman Turkish poet of the 16th-17th centuries known for his panegyric poetry and court verses. He was an influential figure in Ottoman literary tradition.

school: Ottoman Turkish Sufi poet (1618–1694) of the Khalwatiyya order, considered one of the greatest mystical poets in Turkish literature. Exiled twice by the Ottoman authorities for his controversial teachings on wahdat al-wujud, he spent his final years on the island of Lemnos. His ilahis (hymns) are still sung in Sufi gatherings today.
bio: Ottoman Turkish Qalandari Sufi poet and mystic known for his spiritual and devotional poetry. He was an influential figure in Ottoman Sufi literature and mysticism.

school: Qadiriyya (Punjab) — Most prolific Punjabi Sufi poet and one of the most prolific Sufi writers in any language
bio: A 17th-century Punjabi Sufi saint and poet of the Qadiriyya order, venerated in Punjab for his mystical poetry and spiritual teachings.

school: A seventeenth-century Mughal princess and poet, she was the eldest daughter of Emperor Aurangzeb and a renowned patron of literature and the arts. She composed Persian poetry under the pen name Makhfi (Hidden One), producing a diwan noted for its Sufi themes and lyrical beauty. Despite her father's austere religious policies, she maintained a vibrant literary circle and supported scholars and poets at her court.
bio: A 17th-century Mughal princess and accomplished Urdu and Persian poet, daughter of Emperor Aurangzeb, known for her scholarly pursuits and literary contributions.

school: Mughal / Sabk-i Hindi Persian Poetry — Most quoted Persian poet in Afghanistan; Iqbal studied him deeply
bio: A 17th-18th century Persian poet from Delhi known for pioneering a complex, philosophical poetic style called 'Sabk-i Hindi' that influenced Indo-Persian literature.

school: An eighteenth-century Urdu poet from Ahmedabad, Gujarat, he is regarded as one of the pioneers of Urdu ghazal poetry and helped establish Urdu as a serious literary language in the Deccan and northern India. His diwan (collected poetry) demonstrates a refined sensibility that blended Persian poetic conventions with the vernacular traditions of Gujarat. He is often credited with elevating the Urdu ghazal from a regional folk form to a sophisticated literary genre.
bio: A 17th-century Persian poet from Gujarat, credited with pioneering Urdu poetry and establishing Persian literary traditions in the Indian subcontinent.

school: Qadiriyya (Punjab) — His poetry questions religious identity and celebrates divine love; globally popularised by Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan
quotes: Tear down the mosque, tear down the temple — but do not break a human heart.
You have read thousands of books but you have never read yourself.
Where is the need to search? He lives in all.

school: Ahmed Nedim (1681–1730), the poet of Istanbul’s Tulip Era who brought colloquial vitality and urban wit to Ottoman Divan poetry. His ghazals celebrated the pleasures of Istanbul — its gardens, festivals, and romantic encounters — breaking from the traditional Persian-influenced style. He died during the Patrona Halil revolt that ended the Tulip Era.
bio: Ottoman Turkish poet of the 18th century known for his lyrical and romantic verses celebrating love and pleasure. He was a major figure in Ottoman court poetry and Turkish literary tradition.

school: Sindhi Sufism — His shrine in Bhit Shah is Pakistan's most important Sindhi cultural site; founded the raag tradition of Sindhi sacred music
quotes: Love is the only religion — and God is the only beloved.
The river of love has no shore — whoever enters it forgets the bank.
Sing the songs of the soul — the world has enough songs of the self.
Love is the only religion — and God is the only beloved.
The river of love has no shore — whoever enters it forgets the bank.
Sing the songs of the soul — the world has enough songs of the self.

school: Mirza Muhammad Rafi Sauda (1713–1781) was the greatest satirical poet in Urdu literary history and one of the founding figures of classical Urdu poetry. Born in Delhi to a distinguished family, he lived through the catastrophic decline of the Mughal Empire and the sack of Delhi by Nadir Shah and later by Ahmad Shah Abdali. His satirical qasidas and hajviyas savaged the corruption, pretension, and collapse of the Mughal elite with a ferocity unmatched in Urdu literature. He later moved to the court of Awadh in Lucknow, where he continued writing under the patronage of Shuja ud-Dawla. He also composed formal odes and ghazals in the Delhi style. Together with his contemporary Mir Taqi Mir, Sauda established Urdu as a major literary language.
bio: An 18th-century Persian poet and one of the notable literary figures of the Safavid period, known for his mastery of classical Persian poetic forms.

school: Naqshbandiyya — his father founded the Silsila Muhammadiyya; Mir Dard was simultaneously the greatest Urdu poet of 18th-century Delhi and a serious Sufi sheikh, uniquely bridging classical Persian literary tradition and vernacular Urdu mysticism
bio: Naqshbandiyya Sufi poet and spiritual master based in Delhi, known for his devotional poetry and teachings. He was an influential figure in Indian Islamic mysticism and literature.

school: Qadiriyya / Punjabi Sufi Poetry — Heer Ranjha is the Sufi-metaphysical love epic that defines Punjabi cultural identity
quotes: Love is not taught in schools — it is learned through suffering.
The lover asks nothing of the beloved but presence.
Every story of love is the same story — the soul seeking its source.

school: Greatest Urdu poet of the 18th century (1723–1810) who helped establish Urdu as a literary language. Working in Delhi and later Lucknow, his ghazals are marked by a simplicity and emotional depth that set him apart from the more ornamental style of his contemporaries. Ghalib himself acknowledged Mir as the supreme master of Urdu poetry.
quotes: Ask me not what the heart is — for even I do not fully know.
In the court of love, reason has no rank.
I wrote of pain because pain was the most honest thing I knew.

school: Shaykh Galib (1757–1799), the last great poet of Ottoman Divan literature and a master of the Mevlevi (Whirling Dervish) order. His masterpiece Husn u Ashk (Beauty and Love) is considered the finest mesnevî after Rumi’s. He served as shaykh of the Galata Mevlevihanesi in Istanbul and was a friend of Sultan Selim III.
bio: An 18th-century Ottoman Turkish poet and prominent Mawlawi dervish, known for his mystical poetry and spiritual contributions to the Mawlawiyya tradition.

school: Mughal / Urdu Classical Poetry — Witnessed British destruction of Delhi in 1857; his ghazals define classical Urdu literature
quotes: The drop of dew on the petal has no idea it is beautiful — that is what sincerity looks like.
I am not afraid of God’s punishment — I am afraid of a life spent not loving.
Everything is a mirror — the question is what you choose to see in it.

school: Chishti Nizami — the greatest Saraiki Sufi poet; his kafi poetry of separation (wichora) and longing gives voice to the desert landscape of Cholistan; Farid Mela is one of Pakistan's largest cultural festivals
bio: Sindhi and Punjabi Sufi poet renowned for his devotional poetry expressing mystical love of the divine. He remains a venerated figure in Sufi and folk traditions of the Indian subcontinent.

school: Iranian poet and scholar (1886–1951), known as the ‘Poet Laureate’ (Malek al-Sho’ara) of modern Iran. A leading figure in the Constitutional Revolution, he combined classical Persian mastery with modern political themes. His three-volume Sabk-Shinasi (Stylistics) is the definitive history of Persian literary style.
bio: Iranian poet and historian known for his literary works and contributions to Persian cultural discourse during the early modern period. He was an influential figure in early 20th century Persian literature.

school: Iranian poet (1907–1941) considered the greatest female poet of modern Iran. Writing in classical Persian forms, she addressed social justice, women’s rights, and the plight of the poor with clarity and moral force. Though she died young, her Divan remains widely read and studied throughout the Persian-speaking world.
bio: Iranian poet known for her narrative poetry and social critique, particularly addressing women's issues and social justice. She remains one of the most celebrated female Persian poets.

school: Tevfik Kolaylı (March 24, 1879 – January 28, 1953), better known by his pen name Neyzen Tevfik (often misrendered as "Neyzen Teyfik"), was a Turkish poet, satirist, and neyzen (a "ney performer" in Turkish). Tevfik was born in Bodrum and died in Istanbul. In addition to his satire, he composed taksims and saz semais. He used satire against tyranny during the Ottoman period and against those who opposed revolutions during the Republic years. He wrote poems criticising injustice and corruption. He was frequently arrested.
bio: Turkish Mawlawiyya Sufi poet and ney player known for his spiritual and mystical verses. He was a significant figure in Turkish Sufi tradition and music.

school: Abdul Rahim Khan-I-Khana was a Mughal nobleman, military commander, and one of the 'nine jewels' of Emperor Akbar's court. He was equally accomplished in Hindi, Sanskrit, Arabic, and Persian poetry, and his Hindi dohas (couplets) remain among the most widely recited verses in Indian literature. His tomb in Delhi is a notable example of Mughal architecture.
bio: A prominent Mughal military commander and poet of the 16th-17th centuries, known for his contributions to Mughal literature and administration under emperors Akbar and Jahangir.

school: Abdul-Qadir Bedil was the supreme master of the 'Indian style' (sabk-i hindi) in Persian poetry. Born in Patna and based in Delhi, his dense philosophical verses and mystical prose are considered the pinnacle of Persian literary art in the subcontinent. He is revered in Afghanistan and Central Asia as the greatest Persian poet after Hafez, and his Kulliyyat remains one of the largest poetic collections in Persian literature.

Abu Nuwas was the most celebrated poet of the early Abbasid period and the master of the wine poem (khamriyya) in Arabic literature. His bold, irreverent verse broke with pre-Islamic poetic conventions and introduced a new urban sensibility. He appears as a character in the Thousand and One Nights and is considered one of the greatest poets in the Arabic language.
bio: An influential 8th-9th century Abbasid poet known for his innovative use of classical Arabic meters and controversial themes, including wine poetry and homoerotic verses that challenged conservative traditions.
quotes: Bring wine, for abstinence will not last forever

school: Abu Tammam was a major Arab poet of the Abbasid era and the compiler of the celebrated 'Hamasa' anthology of Arabic poetry. His ornate, intellectually complex style (badi) influenced Arabic poetics for centuries and sparked enduring critical debates about innovation versus tradition in poetry. He is ranked alongside al-Mutanabbi and Abu Nuwas among the greatest Arab poets.
bio: An important 9th-century Arab poet of the Abbasid period, known for his eloquent panegyric poetry, innovative imagery, and compilation of the Hamasa anthology of pre-Islamic and Islamic poetry.

school: Al-Hariri of Basra was an Arab poet and prose writer whose 'Maqamat al-Hariri' is one of the greatest works of Arabic literary art. The fifty episodes of rhymed prose featuring the rogue Abu Zayd al-Saruji showcase unmatched mastery of Arabic rhetoric and wordplay. The work was illustrated in famous manuscript editions and influenced prose writing across the Islamic world for centuries.
bio: An 11th-century Arab grammarian and poet famous for authoring the Maqamat al-Hariri, a collection of entertaining literary narratives that became a cornerstone of Arabic literature and rhetoric.

school: Abu-l-Atahiya was an early Abbasid poet who pioneered the genre of ascetic poetry (zuhdiyyat) in Arabic literature. Abandoning courtly panegyrics, he devoted his verse to themes of mortality, worldly renunciation, and divine judgment in a deliberately plain style accessible to ordinary people. His poems became proverbial and influenced later Islamic devotional literature.
bio: An 8th-century Abbasid poet famous for his ascetic and moralistic poetry, known for renouncing worldly pleasures and creating a new style of devotional and ethical verse.

school: Al-Farazdaq was one of the three greatest poets of the Umayyad era, famous for his decades-long poetic rivalry with Jarir. His qaṣīdas are marked by tribal pride, biting satire, and mastery of the classical Arabic poetic tradition. The verbal duel between al-Farazdaq, Jarir, and al-Akhtal is one of the most celebrated episodes in Arabic literary history.
bio: A pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arab poet (641-728) known for his rhetorical brilliance, satirical verse, and bitter poetic rivalry with the poet Jarir that influenced Arabic poetry traditions.

school: Anvari was one of the greatest Persian panegyric poets, celebrated for his elaborate court poetry and his mastery of rhetorical technique. He served at the Seljuq court and his qasidas set the standard for formal Persian poetry. He is traditionally ranked alongside Khaqani and Manuchihri as one of the supreme masters of the qasida form.
bio: A prominent 12th-century Persian poet of the Seljuk era, known for his sophisticated panegyrics, philosophical poetry, and mastery of Persian literary forms.

school: Abu Firas al-Hamdani was a prince of the Hamdanid dynasty and one of the finest Arab poets of the tenth century. His 'Rumiyyat' — poems composed during his captivity in Byzantine Constantinople — are among the most moving works in Arabic literature. He was a cousin of the great patron Sayf al-Dawla and a contemporary of al-Mutanabbi at the Aleppo court.
bio: A 10th-century Arab poet and nobleman from the Hamdanid dynasty, celebrated for his love poetry and verses written during his captivity, influencing classical Arabic poetry.
quotes: I blame my own heart for loving what I cannot have

school: Fakhruddin As'ad Gurgani was a Persian poet who authored 'Vis and Ramin,' the earliest surviving Persian romance epic. Written in the eleventh century, the poem predates and likely influenced the Tristan and Iseult legend in European literature. Its vivid depiction of passionate love and courtly intrigue makes it a cornerstone of Persian narrative poetry.
bio: An 11th-century Persian poet known for his romantic poetry and literary works during the early medieval Persian period.

school: Farrokhi Sistani was a major Persian poet at the court of Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni. He is celebrated for his elegant panegyric qasidas and nature poetry, which rank among the finest in classical Persian literature. His description of spring and the garden became models for later Persian poets.
bio: A 10th-11th century Persian poet from Sistān known for his eloquent panegyric poetry and contributions to classical Persian literary traditions.

school: Hazin Lahiji was a Persian poet and scholar who fled Iran after the Afghan invasion of 1722 and settled in India. His autobiographical memoir and his poetry document the trauma of the Safavid collapse and the life of a Persian literary exile in Mughal India. He is considered one of the last major classical Persian poets.
bio: Persian poet and mystic of the Safavid era known for his lyrical poetry and spiritual works. He was a prominent figure in Persian literary tradition during the 18th century.

school: Ibn al-Rumi was one of the most original poets of the Abbasid period. His poetry is distinguished by extended descriptive passages, psychological depth, and philosophical meditation. He is particularly celebrated for his nature poetry and his poignant elegies on the death of his children, which are considered among the most moving poems in Arabic literature.
bio: Prominent Abbasid poet known for his satirical and panegyric verses, with a sophisticated command of Arabic language and rhetoric. He was one of the most celebrated poets of the 9th century.

school: Jarir ibn Atiyah was one of the three greatest poets of the Umayyad period, renowned for his poetic feuds with al-Farazdaq and al-Akhtal. His command of satire (hija), panegyric, and love poetry was unmatched, and Arabic critics debated for centuries whether he or al-Farazdaq was the greater poet. His verse exemplifies the peak of classical Arabic tribal poetry.
bio: Pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabic poet famous for his panegyric poetry and tribal verses. He was a major figure in the development of Arabic poetic tradition during the Umayyad period.

school: Khwaja Ghulam Farid was the greatest Sufi poet of the Saraiki and Punjabi languages. His kafis express intense mystical longing through the imagery of the desert landscape of southern Punjab. He was also a scholar of Arabic, Persian, and Islamic sciences who attracted a large circle of disciples at his khanqah in Chachran Sharif.
bio: Sindhi and Punjabi Sufi poet renowned for his devotional poetry expressing mystical love of the divine. He remains a venerated figure in Sufi and folk traditions of the Indian subcontinent.

school: Magtymguly Pyragy is the national poet of Turkmenistan and the founding figure of Turkmen literary language. His poems blend Sufi mysticism with themes of Turkmen national identity, unity, and ethical conduct. His work is central to Turkmen cultural identity and in 2024, his poetry was inscribed on the UNESCO Memory of the World Register.
bio: Turkmen Sufi poet considered the national poet of Turkmenistan, known for his mystical verses and spiritual teachings. He was a major figure in Central Asian Islamic poetry.

school: Mahmoud Darwish was the most celebrated Arab poet of the late twentieth century and the literary voice of the Palestinian experience. His poetry evolved from early resistance verse to profound meditations on exile, memory, and love. His works such as 'Memory for Forgetfulness' and 'Mural' are considered masterpieces of modern Arabic literature.
bio: Palestinian poet and literary figure whose work addressed themes of identity, displacement, and resistance, becoming a voice for Palestinian nationalism. He is considered one of the most important Arabic poets of the 20th century.

school: Mehmet Akif Ersoy was a Turkish poet, writer, and Islamic thinker who authored the words of the Turkish national anthem, the 'Istiklal Marsi.' His major work 'Safahat' (Pages) combines Islamic social commentary with vivid depictions of Ottoman life in decline. He was deeply influenced by Muhammad Abduh's reformist thought and advocated for an Islam engaged with modern challenges.
bio: Turkish poet and nationalist figure whose works were influential in Turkish independence and modernization movements. He is best known for writing the lyrics to the Turkish National Anthem.

school: Nizar Qabbani was a Syrian diplomat-poet who became the most popular Arab poet of the twentieth century. His poetry on love, women's freedom, and Arab political consciousness reached an unprecedented mass audience. His works challenged social taboos around female sexuality and desire, earning him both adulation and controversy across the Arab world.
bio: Syrian poet and diplomat known for his romantic and political poetry that often challenged social conventions. He was one of the most popular Arabic poets of the 20th century.

school: Nazim Hikmet was a Turkish poet, playwright, and novelist widely regarded as one of the great poets of the twentieth century. He revolutionised Turkish poetry by introducing free verse and the influence of Soviet futurism. His epic poem 'Human Landscapes from My Country' and his lyrical love poems, composed during his years of imprisonment and exile, have been translated into over fifty languages.
bio: Turkish poet, playwright and political activist whose modernist poetry and leftist ideology made him a controversial but influential literary figure. He is regarded as one of the most important Turkish poets of the 20th century.

school: Rahman Baba is the most beloved poet of Pashto literature. His mystical verses on divine love, compassion, and the vanity of worldly power are recited across the Pashtun homeland. Writing in a clear, accessible style, he brought Sufi themes to ordinary people and his shrine near Peshawar has been a centre of devotion for centuries.
bio: An 17th-century Pashtun Sufi poet and saint from present-day Afghanistan, known for his devotional poetry and spiritual teachings that emphasized love of God and mystical experience.
quotes: Love is the bridge between you and everything

school: Sheikh Ghalib was the last major poet of the Ottoman classical tradition and a sheikh of the Mevlevi order. His allegorical romance 'Husn u Ask' (Beauty and Love) is considered the masterpiece of late Ottoman literature. His dense, intellectually demanding verse and his role as head of the Galata Mevlevihanesi made him a singular figure bridging Ottoman literary culture and Sufi spirituality.
bio: An 18th-century Urdu and Persian Sufi poet from Delhi, considered one of the greatest poets of Urdu literature, known for his complex mystical and philosophical verse.
quotes: Hazar baar socha toh ab bhe kehte ho na kuch khayal

school: Suleyman Celebi was a Turkish poet whose 'Vesilet un-Necat' (Mevlid) is the most widely recited devotional poem in the Turkish-speaking world. Composed in 1409, the poem narrates the birth and life of Prophet Muhammad in accessible, melodious Turkish verse. The Mevlid has been recited at births, deaths, and religious gatherings in Turkey for over six centuries.
bio: A 14th-15th century Ottoman Turkish poet credited with composing the Mevlidi Sherif, a widely recited religious poem about the life of Prophet Muhammad.

school: Wallada bint al-Mustakfi was an Umayyad princess of Cordoba and one of the most celebrated female poets of al-Andalus. She hosted a literary salon that attracted the leading poets and intellectuals of eleventh-century Cordoba. Her love affair with the poet Ibn Zaydun produced some of the most famous love poetry in Arabic literature, and her own satirical verses demonstrate sharp wit and independence.
bio: An 11th-century Andalusian Arab poet and patron of the arts from Córdoba, notable as one of the most celebrated female poets of medieval Islamic Spain.

school: Yahya Kemal Beyatli was a Turkish poet and diplomat who bridged Ottoman literary culture and the modern Turkish Republic. His poetry synthesised classical Ottoman forms with a modern sensibility rooted in Istanbul's history and landscape. He served as a diplomat and parliamentarian, and his poems on Istanbul remain among the most beloved works in Turkish literature.
bio: A 20th-century Turkish poet and literary figure who championed Ottoman cultural heritage and modernized Turkish poetry while preserving classical traditions.

school: Asik Veysel was a blind Turkish folk poet and saz player who became one of the most beloved literary figures of modern Turkey. His poetry, rooted in the Anatolian ashik (minstrel) tradition, celebrates the land, love, and the dignity of rural life. His poem 'Kara Toprak' (Black Earth) is considered one of the finest works of Turkish folk poetry.
bio: A 20th-century Turkish folk poet and musician known for his influential contributions to Turkish folk poetry and traditional musical traditions.
